items.q: What is a brand in marketing?
items.correct: A combination of name, design, symbols and associations that distinguishes a product and creates customer perception and loyalty — more than just a logo.
items.misconception: A brand is just the logo on a product.
items.random_wrong: A brand is the company that makes a product.
items.why: Branding is the sum of perceptions, not just visual identity. Coca-Cola the brand is much more than the script logo — it is associations, taste expectations, advertising history.

items.q: What three Ps are added to make the extended marketing mix (7Ps)?
items.correct: People (staff/service quality), Process (how the service is delivered), and Physical Evidence (tangible cues — uniforms, packaging, branding) — added to make the 4Ps relevant to services marketing.
items.misconception: The 7Ps are just the 4Ps repeated with different words.
items.random_wrong: The 7Ps include Profit, Planning and Performance.
items.why: The extended mix specifically addresses services, where customer experience depends on staff, process and atmospherics — not just product. Students treat 4Ps and 7Ps as interchangeable, missing what services marketing requires.

items.q: What four growth strategies does Ansoff's Matrix identify?
items.correct: Market penetration (existing product, existing market), market development (existing product, new market), product development (new product, existing market), diversification (new product, new market).
items.misconception: Ansoff's Matrix is about analysing competitors.
items.random_wrong: Ansoff's Matrix shows market share against profit.
items.why: Students confuse Ansoff (growth strategy) with Porter's Five Forces (competition) or BCG Matrix (portfolio). Ansoff is specifically about the four product/market combinations for growth.

items.q: Designing a product to occupy a specific place in the buyer's mind is:
items.why: It's Positioning. “Segmentation” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which life cycle stage sees sales falling and may prompt withdrawal?
items.why: It's Decline. “Introduction” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many classic stages in the product life cycle?
items.why: Exact answer: 4. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
